content_markdown:
Two flasks P and Q contain an ideal gas and are connected with a tube of negligible volume compared to that of the flasks. The volume of P is twice the volume of Q.

P is held at a temperature of $200\text{ K}$ and Q is held at a temperature of $400\text{ K}$.

What is $\frac{\text{mass of gas in P}}{\text{mass of gas in Q}}$?

diagram_metadata.description: Two spherical flasks, a larger one labeled P and a smaller one labeled Q, connected by a thin horizontal tube.

vibe_explanation: Since the flasks are connected, the pressure $P$ is the same in both. Using the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$, the number of moles is $n = \frac{PV}{RT}$. The mass is proportional to the number of moles for the same gas. For flask P: $n_P \propto \frac{V_P}{T_P} = \frac{2V_Q}{200}$. For flask Q: $n_Q \propto \frac{V_Q}{T_Q} = \frac{V_Q}{400}$. Taking the ratio $\frac{n_P}{n_Q} = \frac{2/200}{1/400} = \frac{1/100}{1/400} = 4$.
